lecture.New_Day4Part1audio01.mp3: SQL supports standard arithmetic operators that you can use in SELECT expressions, WHERE conditions, and ORDER BY clauses. They operate on numeric data types.
lecture.New_Day4Part1audio02.mp3: Now, let's look at the five core arithmetic operators in action. First, the plus sign performs addition — like adding a ten-thousand bonus to a base salary. Next, the minus sign handles subtraction — deducting five thousand for expenses. The asterisk is used for multiplication — like applying a ten percent salary raise with one-point-one. The forward slash performs division — dividing annual salary by twelve to find monthly pay. And finally, the percent sign is the modulo operator — which gives you the remainder left over after division.
lecture.New_Day4Part1audio03.mp3: Great — now let's put those operators to work in real queries. In the first one, we pull each employee's first name and salary, then compute two new columns directly in the SELECT: dividing salary by twelve-point-zero gives us the monthly pay, and multiplying salary by zero-point-ten gives the annual bonus. The second query targets the products table and derives gross profit simply by subtracting cost price from unit price. Notice how both computations happen right inside the SELECT — you're building new columns on the fly, without touching the original data at all.
lecture.New_Day4Part1audio04.mp3: Now here is a subtle but dangerous trap you must watch out for — integer division. When you divide two whole numbers in SQLite, the engine throws away the decimal part entirely. So seven divided by two gives you three, not three-point-five. The fix is one small change: write twelve-point-zero instead of just twelve. That single decimal forces the engine into floating-point mode and you get the precise result. It is a tiny detail with a huge impact on accuracy, and exactly the kind of thing that causes silent data errors in production systems.
lecture.New_Day4Part1audio05.mp3: So far we have been working with individual arithmetic operators. But what happens when you mix multiple operators in a single expression — or combine arithmetic with logical conditions like AND and OR? That is where operator precedence becomes absolutely critical. SQL evaluates every expression following a strict internal order, and if you are unaware of it, your queries can silently return completely wrong results — no error, no warning, just bad data. Let us break that order down exactly.
lecture.New_Day4Part1audio06.mp3: Here is the complete precedence hierarchy from highest to lowest binding power. At the very top sit parentheses — whatever you wrap in parentheses is evaluated first, unconditionally. Next come the multiplicative operators: multiplication, division, and modulo. Then addition and subtraction. After arithmetic come the comparison operators — equals, not equals, less than, greater than. Then logical NOT, then AND, and at the absolute bottom — with the lowest binding power of all — sits OR. Burn this into memory: AND binds tighter than OR. That single fact is behind more silent production bugs than almost anything else in SQL.
lecture.New_Day4Part1audio07.mp3: Let me show you exactly why this matters with a real example. Look at the first query — no parentheses. You might read it as: give me employees from department ten or twenty, where salary exceeds sixty thousand. But SQL does not read it that way. Because AND binds tighter than OR, the engine parses it as: give me everyone in department ten — all of them, regardless of salary — plus employees in department twenty who earn over sixty thousand. That is a completely different result set. And the query runs perfectly without any error message. The second version wraps the OR in parentheses, making the intent explicit and the result correct. One pair of parentheses — entirely different outcome.
lecture.New_Day4Part1audio08.mp3: This is exactly the kind of bug that slips into production and quietly lives there for months. The query runs, returns data, and nobody questions it — because it looks right at a glance. So make this your permanent habit: whenever you mix AND and OR in the same condition, always use parentheses. They cost absolutely nothing at runtime — the optimizer removes redundant ones — but they make your intent unmistakable to anyone reading the code, including your future self. Think of parentheses as free insurance against silent correctness bugs.
lecture.New_Day4Part1audio09.mp3: Moving forward, let us talk about two powerful but often misunderstood operators — ALL and ANY. Both allow you to compare a single value against a full set of values returned by a subquery. They extend the comparison operators you already know — greater than, less than, equals — and let you ask much richer questions about your data without hardcoding any specific values. They appear regularly in SQL interviews, so understanding them deeply will set you apart.
lecture.New_Day4Part1audio10.mp3: Greater than ANY means: is this value greater than at least one value in the subquery result — in other words, greater than the minimum. If you ask which employees earn more than ANY salary in the Marketing department, you get everyone who earns more than the lowest Marketing salary. That is the threshold. In our sandbox, since SQLite does not support ANY directly, you rewrite this as: salary greater than SELECT MIN of salary from Marketing. Same logic, identical result set, wider compatibility.
lecture.New_Day4Part1audio11.mp3: Greater than ALL is the stronger version — it means: is this value greater than every single value in the subquery result, which is the same as greater than the maximum. So if you want employees who out-earn every Sales employee — not just some of them, but all of them — you use greater than ALL. In SQLite, this becomes: salary greater than SELECT MAX of salary from Sales. ALL sets the bar at the very top of the list, while ANY sets it at the very bottom. Two operators, opposite thresholds.
lecture.New_Day4Part1audio12.mp3: One important clarification here. ALL and ANY are part of the SQL standard and are supported natively in both MySQL and PostgreSQL. SQLite, however, does not implement them — so any ALL or ANY query you try to run in our sandbox will fail with an error. When working here, always rewrite them using aggregates: SELECT MIN for ANY, and SELECT MAX for ALL. In a real production database on MySQL or PostgreSQL, you can use ALL and ANY directly without any rewriting.
lecture.New_Day4Part1audio13.mp3: And here are four equivalences that are absolutely worth memorising, because they will come up in SQL interviews. Equals ANY is identical to IN — they are fully interchangeable. Not equal ALL is identical to NOT IN. Greater than ANY is the same as greater than SELECT MIN. And greater than ALL is the same as greater than SELECT MAX. These four mappings let you instantly translate between different SQL styles and understand what any query is doing the moment you see ALL or ANY in someone else's code.
lecture.New_Day4Part1audio14.mp3: Now let us look at a scenario that catches many developers completely off guard — searching for literal percent signs or underscores inside text data. Normally, the LIKE operator treats percent as a wildcard matching any sequence of characters, and underscore as a wildcard matching exactly one character. But what if your actual data contains a percent sign — like a discount field storing values such as fifty percent? You need a way to tell SQL: this percent is literal text, not a wildcard. That is precisely what the ESCAPE clause does.
lecture.New_Day4Part1audio15.mp3: Take the first example — we want to find products whose name contains the literal text fifty percent, with the actual percent sign embedded in it. The pattern is: percent fifty exclamation-percent percent, with ESCAPE exclamation declared at the end. The exclamation mark is our chosen escape character, so exclamation followed by percent means: treat this as a real percent sign, not a wildcard. The second query applies the exact same technique to underscores — finding employee emails that contain an actual underscore character rather than treating it as a single-character wildcard. One pattern, complete control.
lecture.New_Day4Part1audio16.mp3: One important clarification: there is no built-in default escape character in standard SQL. You must always declare it explicitly using ESCAPE followed by your chosen character in single quotes. The most common choices are backslash, exclamation mark, or hash — pick whichever character is least likely to appear in your actual data. Once declared, that character also escapes itself: two exclamation marks in a row match a single literal exclamation mark. Choose one convention for your codebase and apply it consistently.
lecture.New_Day4Part1audio17.mp3: We have covered arithmetic, precedence, ALL and ANY, and escaping wildcards. Now we arrive at arguably the most important concept in this entire lesson — and one of the most misunderstood ideas in all of SQL. NULL. NULL is not zero. NULL is not an empty string. NULL means the value is simply unknown. And the moment NULL enters an arithmetic expression or a comparison, something very specific and consequential happens — it propagates silently through everything it touches.
lecture.New_Day4Part1audio18.mp3: Look at this query carefully. We compute total compensation by adding salary and commission. For employees who have a commission value, the addition works perfectly. But for employees where commission is NULL — where the commission is simply unknown — the entire total_comp result becomes NULL too. That is NULL propagation in action. The fix is COALESCE: wrapping commission in COALESCE with a fallback of zero tells SQL to substitute zero wherever commission is absent. Now the calculation works correctly for every single row. The filtering section shows the same principle — a plain greater-than comparison silently excludes NULL commission rows because the comparison evaluates to UNKNOWN. Adding OR commission IS NULL explicitly brings those employees back into the result.
lecture.New_Day4Part1audio19.mp3: Let me state this clearly because it is one of the most critical rules in all of SQL: NULL is not zero, and NULL is not equal to NULL. Salary plus NULL is NULL — not salary. NULL equals NULL evaluates to UNKNOWN — not TRUE. You cannot use the equals sign to check for NULL, ever. The only correct way to test whether a value is absent is IS NULL or IS NOT NULL. Any other comparison involving NULL silently produces UNKNOWN, and UNKNOWN rows are filtered out by WHERE without any error or warning. That silence is precisely what makes NULL bugs so difficult to catch.
lecture.New_Day4Part1audio20.mp3: That brings us to three-valued logic — the formal framework that explains exactly why NULL behaves the way it does. Most programming languages operate with two logical values: true and false. SQL operates with three: TRUE, FALSE, and UNKNOWN. UNKNOWN is the result you get whenever NULL appears in any predicate. The WHERE clause has one simple rule: it keeps a row only when the predicate is TRUE. If the result is FALSE, the row is discarded. If the result is UNKNOWN, the row is also discarded — silently, exactly as if it were FALSE. This treatment of UNKNOWN is the root of almost every NULL-related surprise you will ever encounter in SQL.
lecture.New_Day4Part1audio21.mp3: The truth table makes every case concrete. Five equals five is TRUE — that is straightforward. Five equals NULL is UNKNOWN — because NULL means we do not know the value, so we cannot confirm equality. NULL equals NULL is also UNKNOWN — comparing two unknowns does not produce certainty. NOT UNKNOWN stays UNKNOWN — you cannot flip an unknown into something definite. TRUE AND UNKNOWN collapses to UNKNOWN — because the overall outcome depends on the unknown value. And FALSE OR UNKNOWN is UNKNOWN — not definitively true. Every single UNKNOWN result causes WHERE to silently discard the row.
lecture.New_Day4Part1audio22.mp3: Now let us see exactly how this plays out in the most notorious NULL bug in SQL — the NOT IN trap. This query asks for employees whose salary does not appear in the list of commissions from the subquery. That sounds perfectly reasonable. But the commission column contains NULL values for employees who have no commission. When NOT IN encounters even a single NULL anywhere in that subquery result, it expands the condition into a chain of not-equal comparisons against every value including NULL — and every single comparison against NULL evaluates to UNKNOWN. The final result: zero rows returned. Not because there are no matching employees — but because every comparison silently collapses to UNKNOWN and WHERE discards all of them.
lecture.New_Day4Part1audio23.mp3: This is one of the most dangerous bugs in SQL precisely because the query runs without any error or warning — it simply returns empty results. And empty results often look like correct results, especially during development. The fix is straightforward: either add WHERE commission IS NOT NULL inside the subquery to filter out NULLs before NOT IN sees them — or better yet, rewrite the entire query using NOT EXISTS, which evaluates row by row and is inherently NULL-safe by design. Make this your rule: every time you write NOT IN with a subquery, ask yourself — could that subquery ever return a NULL? If the answer is yes — or even possibly — use NOT EXISTS instead. That single habit will save you from some of the most frustrating debugging sessions of your SQL career.

questions.New_Day4Question01.mp3: Let us start with a practical arithmetic challenge. Write a query that shows every employee's monthly pay breakdown — their first name, their annual salary, and a computed column that divides that salary by twelve to get the monthly figure. Label the computed column monthly_salary.
questions.New_Day4Question01sol.mp3: Simple and clean — one division in the SELECT does everything. We pull first_name and salary as-is, then divide salary by twelve-point-zero and alias it monthly_salary. SELECT first_name, salary, salary divided by twelve point zero AS monthly_salary FROM employees.
questions.New_Day4Question02.mp3: Now move to the products table. Calculate the gross profit for every product — that is unit price minus cost price. Return the product name, unit price, cost price, and the computed gross profit, then order the results from the highest profit down to the lowest.
questions.New_Day4Question02sol.mp3: Subtraction at its most practical. Pull name, unit_price, and cost_price directly, then compute the difference as gross_profit. The ORDER BY descending brings your highest-margin products straight to the top. SELECT name, unit_price, cost_price, unit_price minus cost_price AS gross_profit FROM products ORDER BY gross_profit descending.
questions.New_Day4Question03.mp3: Here is where NULL awareness becomes essential. Some employees have no commission — their commission field is NULL. Write a query that computes total compensation by adding salary to commission, but treats any missing commission as zero rather than NULL. Return first_name, salary, commission, and the computed total_comp column.
questions.New_Day4Question03sol.mp3: The key move here is COALESCE. Wrap commission in it with a fallback of zero — so any NULL commission becomes zero before the addition happens, and total_comp is always a number, never NULL. SELECT first_name, salary, commission, salary plus COALESCE of commission comma zero AS total_comp FROM employees.
questions.New_Day4Question04.mp3: This one tests your precedence awareness directly. Find employees who belong to department ten or twenty AND earn more than seventy thousand. The key requirement: you must wrap the OR condition in explicit parentheses to guarantee SQL evaluates it as intended. Return all columns.
questions.New_Day4Question04sol.mp3: The parentheses are doing all the critical work here. They group both department conditions together so AND applies to both at once — not just to department twenty alone. SELECT star FROM employees WHERE open paren department_id equals ten OR department_id equals twenty close paren AND salary greater than seventy thousand.
questions.New_Day4Question05.mp3: Write a query that finds every employee who earns more than every single person in the Sales department — department forty. Use a MAX subquery to find the ceiling salary in Sales, then return all employees whose salary clears that ceiling. Get first_name and salary.
questions.New_Day4Question05sol.mp3: The ceiling comes from a nested SELECT MAX. Once you have the highest Sales salary as a single value, the outer query simply filters everyone above it. SELECT first_name, salary FROM employees WHERE salary greater than open paren SELECT MAX of salary FROM employees WHERE department_id equals forty close paren.
questions.New_Day4Question06.mp3: Your client wants to preview what prices would look like after applying an eighteen percent markup. Write a query against the products table that returns each product's name alongside a markup_price column — unit price multiplied by one-point-one-eight.
questions.New_Day4Question06sol.mp3: As straightforward as it gets — one multiplication, one alias, one table. SELECT name, unit_price times one point one eight AS markup_price FROM products.
questions.New_Day4Question07.mp3: Here is a NULL filtering challenge. Write a query that captures two groups of employees in a single result: those who earn a commission above five thousand, and those who have no commission recorded at all. Return first_name and commission for both groups.
questions.New_Day4Question07sol.mp3: Two conditions joined by OR — that is the entire solution. The first catches high-commission earners, the second explicitly reclaims everyone with no commission at all. SELECT first_name, commission FROM employees WHERE commission greater than five thousand OR commission IS NULL.
questions.New_Day4Question08.mp3: Divide-by-zero can crash your queries silently. Write a query that calculates profit margin for each product — unit price minus cost price, divided by unit price — but use NULLIF to safely handle any product where unit price is zero, converting the denominator to NULL instead of causing an error. Label the result as margin.
questions.New_Day4Question08sol.mp3: NULLIF in the denominator is the safeguard. If unit_price is zero, NULLIF converts it to NULL, which turns the entire division into NULL — no crash, no error, just a safe missing value. SELECT name, open paren unit_price minus cost_price close paren times one point zero divided by NULLIF of unit_price comma zero AS margin FROM products.
questions.New_Day4Question09.mp3: Take the margin concept one step further. For each product, compute the profit as a percentage: unit price minus cost price, multiplied by one hundred, divided by unit price, and round the final result to two decimal places. Label it profit_pct.
questions.New_Day4Question09sol.mp3: Wrap the numerator in parentheses to ensure subtraction happens before multiplication, then let ROUND trim the result to two decimal places. SELECT name, ROUND of open paren unit_price minus cost_price close paren times one hundred point zero divided by unit_price comma two AS profit_pct FROM products.
questions.New_Day4Question10.mp3: Flip the comparison from the Sales challenge. Now find employees whose salary exceeds at least one person's salary in the Marketing department — department thirty. Use a MIN subquery to find that lower bound, then return the first_name and salary of everyone who clears it.
questions.New_Day4Question10sol.mp3: Same structure as the Sales challenge, but this time MIN sets the floor. Anyone who earns above the lowest Marketing salary qualifies — and that is a much wider group. SELECT first_name, salary FROM employees WHERE salary greater than open paren SELECT MIN of salary FROM employees WHERE department_id equals thirty close paren.
questions.New_Day4Question11.mp3: Put the modulo operator to use. Write a query that returns only products with an even product ID — meaning product ID leaves zero remainder when divided by two. Return product_id and name.
questions.New_Day4Question11sol.mp3: One condition, one operator. If the remainder after dividing product_id by two is zero, the ID is even — and that is all you need. SELECT product_id, name FROM products WHERE product_id modulo two equals zero.
questions.New_Day4Question12.mp3: Your final challenge. For each product, calculate its total stock value by multiplying stock quantity by cost price. Return the product name and the computed stock_value, but only include products where that value exceeds one hundred thousand.
questions.New_Day4Question12sol.mp3: Notice that the multiplication appears twice — once in SELECT to display it, and once in WHERE to filter on it. In production you would avoid repeating it by using a subquery, but here it reads clearly and runs correctly. SELECT name, stock_qty times cost_price AS stock_value FROM products WHERE stock_qty times cost_price greater than one hundred thousand.
